topic.title_cn: 职场自我介绍与角色说明
topic.prompt_en: Describe how you would introduce yourself and your role at work.
topic.learning_goal: Learn to describe your work identity through role, scope, and practical contribution.

angles.title: Set the work context
content: Say what the work situation is and what your role is.
That gives the listener a frame before the details begin.

angles.title: Explain the issue or task
content: Describe the task, bug, delay, or concept in practical terms.
Avoid jargon if the topic is about clear communication.

angles.title: Clarify responsibility
content: Show what belongs to you, to the team, or to the process.
That keeps the answer professional instead of defensive.

angles.title: End with the next step
content: Finish with what you did or plan to do next.
A work answer should feel forward-looking.

sequence.target: State the role, project, or workplace situation clearly.
Explain what happened and how you handled it.
End with responsibility, learning, or action.

high_score_tips: Workplace answers sound stronger when they focus on clarity, not self-praise.
Explain the practical effect of a problem, not just the technical label.
Responsibility language should sound calm and specific.
End with the next step or lesson so the answer feels professional.

content.why_it_hurts: A workplace answer loses impact if the listener cannot understand the main point.
content.fix: Translate the issue into plain effect, such as delay, confusion, or customer impact.

content.why_it_hurts: The answer sounds weaker if you only try to avoid blame.
content.fix: Explain responsibility honestly and move quickly to what you did next.

content.why_it_hurts: Work answers feel incomplete if the story stops at the problem.
content.fix: End with the next step, fix, or communication follow-up.

language.reason: Happen is intransitive and does not take passive form.

language.better_example: I explained the problem to him.
language.reason: Explain usually takes the thing before the person with to.

core.example: I would naturally use "role" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "deadline" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "issue" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "update" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "team" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "responsibility" when talking about this topic.

extended.note: Useful for bug explanations and practical analysis.
Natural for progress and delay reports.
Useful when translating a work issue into plain language.

core.example: I would use "I am responsible for" naturally in this answer.
I would use "at the moment" naturally in this answer.
I would use "the main issue is" naturally in this answer.
I would use "the next step is" naturally in this answer.
I would use "to put it simply" naturally in this answer.
I would use "take responsibility for" naturally in this answer.

extended.phrase: from a practical point of view
extended.example: I would use "from a practical point of view" when making the answer sound more natural.
I would use "what I did next was" when making the answer sound more natural.

extended.phrase: the impact on the team was
extended.example: I would use "the impact on the team was" when making the answer sound more natural.

extended.phrase: I wanted to make it clear that
extended.example: I would use "I wanted to make it clear that" when making the answer sound more natural.

framework.goal: Handle workplace topics through context, issue, responsibility, and next step.

moves: Set the work context and your role.
Explain the issue, update, or concept in practical language.
State how you responded or communicated.
Clarify ownership and the next action.
Keep the ending constructive and professional.

samples.answer:
The kind of self-introduction that explains what i actually do is something I can talk about quite naturally. When I introduce myself at work, I try to say more than just my job title because titles alone are often too vague. I usually explain which team I work with, what kind of work I handle most often, and how my role connects with other people. That way, the other person quickly understands not only what I do, but also when it makes sense to involve me. A good work introduction should sound clear and useful rather than self-promotional.

What made it worth talking about was not only the event or idea itself, but also the effect it had on the way I think or act. The goal is simply to make collaboration easier from the start. Overall, it is a topic that feels real to me because it connects to ordinary life rather than a perfect story.
highlights: Band 6 answer matched to the 职场自我介绍与角色说明 topic
Keeps spoken rhythm while staying specific and teachable

band_guide.focus: Clear work context and next step
opening: The topic that comes to mind is the kind of self-introduction that explains what I actually do.
I would first explain the background clearly.
body: Then I would add the central detail or turning point.
After that, I would show the result or practical effect.
closing: Finally, I would end with the takeaway or present view.

upgrades.improved: It mattered because The goal is simply to make collaboration easier from the start.
upgrades.why: A specific reason sounds much more natural than a general claim.
upgrades.note: Use the personal effect to close the answer.

upgrades.improved: That way, the other person quickly understands not only what I do, but also when it makes sense to involve me.
upgrades.why: A clear turning point gives the answer shape and movement.
upgrades.note: This is especially useful in spoken storytelling and reflective answers.

samples.answer:
The topic that comes to mind for me is the kind of self-introduction that explains what I actually do. When I introduce myself at work, I try to say more than just my job title because titles alone are often too vague. I usually explain which team I work with, what kind of work I handle most often, and how my role connects with other people. What stood out most was that that way, the other person quickly understands not only what I do, but also when it makes sense to involve me.

After that, a good work introduction should sound clear and useful rather than self-promotional. What I find interesting about it now is that the situation seemed fairly ordinary at the time, but it revealed something more lasting about how I handle pressure, choices, or relationships. The goal is simply to make collaboration easier from the start. So if I were explaining this in a speaking test, I would focus less on sounding dramatic and more on why the experience or idea genuinely stayed with me.

samples.answer:
If I had to answer this topic in a more reflective way, I would still begin with the kind of self-introduction that explains what I actually do. When I introduce myself at work, I try to say more than just my job title because titles alone are often too vague. I usually explain which team I work with, what kind of work I handle most often, and how my role connects with other people. The turning point, for me, was that that way, the other person quickly understands not only what I do, but also when it makes sense to involve me.

From there, a good work introduction should sound clear and useful rather than self-promotional. What matters now is not the surface event or statement alone, but the shift in perspective behind it. The goal is simply to make collaboration easier from the start. In that sense, the topic feels useful because it shows not only what happened or what I think, but also how my judgment has become a little more precise over time.

practice.task: Describe how you would introduce yourself and your role at work.
checklist: State the work context and your role.
Explain the issue or concept in clear language.

self_prompts: What was the actual issue or task?
